title: 2025/10/08 - EW
description: Education and Workforce Committee | Wednesday 10 October 2025

chunks.text: To, uh, present your petition, uh, to the committee and speak on it. We've got you, uh, with us this morning for 15 minutes. Mm-hmm. Uh, including any time for questions. So it's up to you how you want to use the time. So I'll hand over to you to start to introduce yourself to the committee. Okay. Um, modern everyone. My name is Tuda and I'm a 15-year-old student at Wat College in West Auckland.

Um, I've been involved, sorry. It's All good. Matilda, start again. Is it, Is it good? No. Uh, you know, the mic is for anyone online. It doesn't actually include the volume in the room, so the more you speak up, the more everyone can hear. Okay. Yeah. Okay. I'll go again. Um, more than everyone.

My name is Matilda and I'm a year 11 student at Makati College in West Stockland. I've been involved in this issue for about a year and a half after noticing the positive impacts the free school lunch program had on schools and in students in particular, um, after the budget cuts this year, the impacts on students at schools was astounding.

The amount of lunches eaten dropped from 80% in our school to 50% because of the budget cuts to the lunches. It's more cost effective to provide good quality meals that provide students with enough nutrition to learn than with poor quality meals that do not. Students struggle to learn on an empty stomach, and that inhibits the chances of being able to contribute to society in the workforce 'cause of this chance of passing.

NCEA, the scheme has been proven to work in other OECD countries like the uk, France, Japan, and the USA, which have universal preschool lunch schemes. In those countries, they spend around $6 per student, which gives around 30% of the daily nutrition needs international to the international standard. In New Zealand, the $3 lunch program only provides 18% of daily nutrition, and that is not enough for a student to learn.

The prac that these other OECD countries are providing free school lunches to university to all students, proves that they're good value for money, and that they see the value they have for the workforce and for the country at large, the $3 lunches are not working. They do not meet the bar to help student education. Instead, we should follow the lead of the other OECD countries because it is proven to help students learn better when the nutritional needs are met.

That requires $6 per lunch. This government has put a lot of money into attendance rates, providing free school lunches takes away barriers from students attending school when they might not get food at home. There are also other benefits. There's a mental health. When the AKA Pro program was introduced, there was a mental health increase amongst already food insecure individuals from between nine and 20%.

And because mental health is such a difficult, complex problem to solve something that can provide an in mental health increase of 20% is astounding. And a mental health crisis. After living in a community like Westland for my whole life, and seeing people in poverty and being friends with people in poverty, going to school with them, you can see the impacts it has, and you can see how much this means to them.

But the three school lunch, three school lunches, the $3 ones don't, they just don't work. And it's sending these people who need the lunches more than anybody else into more poverty. And yeah. Ians is there anything else you'd like to add before we go To? No questions. The questions?

Okay, great. Um, Shannon Kilda, um, thank you very much for your contribution today. I think it just sends the message that the government doesn't care enough about us to invest in money. Invest money that does real good. But as a student, you can see it in school, you can see it in the community, you can see the impacts. So yeah, I really think it sends the message that the government doesn't care, doesn't, isn't doing the things that they need to do to help students' education. Especially when you're talking about attendance rates. They're talking about NCA and it feels like this is a really good way to tackle problems like that. But then they stash the budget and, yeah, Builder. Thank you.

I couldn't say personally, I don't eat the lunches very often 'cause I don't, they don't taste good enough to eat a lot of the time, so you'd probably have to ask other students and see what they think to get their views. I don't know, personally, look Full trip. Yeah. Thanks Matilda. Um, you know your fellow students and you've been following this issue the whole time, um, over the last year and a half.

What do they, what do the students, if, if they were all here, let's say the room was full of your fellow students Yeah. And we asked them what they think of food since the cuts were made, what would they say? Well, I did a survey recently of students at my school, and I got some comments at the bottom of the survey. The smell is rancid. Um, one of them said in medieval times, this would be a punishment, having to eat chalky curry and bread like this. At my school, um, because the deputy principal, who's the pastoral, um, leader, she changes the quota we received to fit demand.

And last year with the program, there were 80% of students were being provided for with the lunches to fit demand. But this year that's gone down to 50%, which I think really shows students aren't eating them, and that really does have impacts on their education and their future work outcomes.

Everything. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, Matilda, I, I know your school well, but could you, um, uh, give the committee a bit of a sense of the socioeconomic Yeah. Kind of range in the school? So it's a D South three school in Henderson, west Auckland. We have the 2000 students.

It's, the population's been really expanding recently. Um, it's, I think 50% is Maori Pacifica, 25% European, and then 25% other ethnicities. So yeah, it's really broad range. Yeah. Sad. Sadly, in Fact isn't true in it. Yeah, it is true. And because the new lunches only provide 18% of their daily nutrition, that's might be the only meal they get that day. Only 18% of their nutrition is delivered, and that's not enough. Thanks, Matilda, for coming to the committee and presenting a couple of questions coming out of, uh, things that you've said today.

One is, how do you feel about the fact that there are significantly larger numbers of students across New Zealand that are receiving the lunches this year compared to last year because, uh, there's been able to be a spread of the number of students getting it because of the work done by the minister? Yeah, I think that's really good and I think that's what it should be.

But I think the lunches that are delivered should be at a high enough quality with students who actually eat them and enjoy them and positively benefit off them. Because if lunches are being delivered to students who won't eat them, that's just a waste of food and time and money. And so, yeah, I think it's good that it's being spread. And in other countries like that, provide them, like the UK Japan who provide universal preschool lunches, they give it to every single student in their, in their country.

And I think that's really significant and I think that's something we should try and follow, but with lunches that are high enough standard for students to actually eat. Thank you. Do you know what the attendance, attendance rates are at your school and what's happened over the last year? I do not know. Sorry. Okay. We spoke Too much.

Oh, yeah. Um, so she said, Ms. Fugman is my deputy principal. Having the school lunches. So those that, what are those children doing that weren't students doing that? Weren't that now aren't, are they bringing their own lunch to school or what's happening? A lot of people, they wouldn't, a lot of people don't bring their own lunch to school, so they just wouldn't be eating much at all because tuk shop's quite expensive, so people will just go hungry over the day.

A lot of people don't eat breakfast either, so it really is like, you'd, you'd get to school and maybe you'd have take some food for someone else and then you'd go home and have dinner and that would be it. So, yeah. Okay. Now other questions? Good submission. Thank you so much for taking the time.

Thank you for, come down to Wellington and to present to the committee, uh, on your petition. All done on the work. Mahi, you've done the petition. Thank you so much. Done committee. We're now moving, um, to the petition of Catherine. Uh, five minutes. Sure. Can we do some follow up? Okay. So, um, I can ask Catherine to join us, um, at the table, the middle here.

Morning, Catherine. We'll just wait for the committee clerk Yeah. To come back in. No problem. And, um, we've got 15 minutes with you this morning. Thank you. Uh, including any time for questions. Yes. So I'll hand over to you. Uh, feel free to add to your submission, uh, to your petition, uh, anything that you'd like, and then mm-hmm. Um, some questions would be Great. Yeah. Thank you. Uh, so my name is Catherine Lamont, and I'm the initiator of the petition calling for equal a CC coverage to volunteer firefighters.

Uh, the petition was supported by 36 and a half thousand New Zealanders, uh, and it was presented and tabled in parliament on the 6th of May. The petition comes from both lived experience and widespread community concern. Uh, I started this after witnessing, uh, the devastating impact that PTSD had on a local volunteer filing.

Um, sorry. Uh, he was left without financial support and without the ability to work because of his PTSD diagnosis. And he did not qualify under our current a CC coverage. Uh, volunteer firefighters are the backbone of fire and emergency. They make up 86% of FES workforce.

They cover 93% of our land mass, and they attend to the majority of series incidents. So into 2023, volunteers attended 70% of all motor vehicle accidents, 71% of medical emergencies, and 81% of vegetation fires. They are carrying the workload for fes, Despite carrying the same risks, the same responsibilities, and completing the same core training as their paid via as their paid counterparts.

They are excluded from full a CC protections, and it is not a sustainable way forward. Uh, this petition really speaks to fairness and equality. So volunteers are doing the same training as their paid firefighters. They are held to the same professional standards across their communities, and we ask them to serve in a professional way, and we must protect them through our laws.

They respond to the same high risk incidents. They are exposed to the same carcinogens, they carry the same psychological burden, and yet under a CC, they are excluded from cover for the gradual process injuries, occupational illnesses and mental trauma. Trauma does not differentiate between paid and unpaid roles.

Volunteer firefighters are essential to New Zealand's emergency response. They are not a nice to have. They provide an estimated $800 million worth of service annually and unpaid labor hours, a contribution that would be impossible to replace for the fully paid workforce.

Our volunteer firefighters are the first responders in their communities, and without them, large parts of New Zealand would be left without effective foreign emergency coverage. And if we continue to ask our volunteer firefighters to bear an unfair personal cost, we risk losing them and without them a cornerstone of our emergency response system.
This petition speaks to the value that we place on volunteer firefighters.

They are expected and required. New Zealanders expect our volunteer firefighters to respond 24 hours of the day, seven days a week, 365 days of the year. They are formally recognized in the Foreign Emergency New Zealand Act as personnel of funds alongside employees and contractors.

The FES Act already recognizes the unique role of volunteers and the A CC Act should align with this. There is no other volunteer group in New Zealand that has this formal recognition within a Crown entity and this unique status justifies ring fencing these people within the A CC Act.

There is overwhelming public and political support for this change. It is not a partisan issue. It is about fairness, recogni recognition, and doing the right thing. If we continue to ask volunteers to pay this high personal price for being volunteer firemen, and we continue to leave them unprotected in crucial areas, they may cease volunteering or people may be reluctant to volunteer in the first place.

I am ready for your Questions, Vanessa. Thank you, chair. Um, thank you so much for your submission, and I agree there's with so many elements of, um, of what you've put forward. Mm. Um, this was something I was, you know, looking at behind the scenes as, you know, ideas for members bills and things like that. Um, the things that, um, tripped me up along the way mm-hmm.

Was the, um, the potential broadening of, um, the scope of a CC, um, because, um, doing that frankly increases liability. Um, and also it's, it wouldn't, it would be very difficult to ring fence just, um, volunteer firefighters, um, without including, um, volunteer paramedics.

Um, also, um, other health professionals that work, um, that are called in, um, various things like that. So this, it's, um, although I can see how you would, um, see it limited to advice, I think that, um, can you understand that it would be potentially quite hard to limit it.

The issue that I'm here to speak to today Yeah. Is, is the volunteer firefighter Aspect. Yeah. Yeah. And so, um, the, the, as the thing is that it potentially is quite hard to do that, um, without then creating the precedent or the, the creep that then carries on. Um, and so the, it does create liability and that's pars partially why there may have been some reluctance in the past to do this.

Um, yeah. So that's just my point and my question, I guess. Yeah. Okay. Any other questions? Yeah, Palm sheet Palm. Yeah. So you mentioned, uh, gradual, uh, injury. Mm. And, uh, uh, are you like, uh, are you, uh, be able to be a bit more specific, what you're trying to cover, what you're trying to advocate for in the gradual injuries?

Um, you've mentioned mental health mm-hmm. Occupational illness, but then can you be a bit more specific for our understanding? Yes, of course. And I think Bill may be a better placed to expand on that. Is that acceptable for me to comment? Yeah, thank you. Well, the, um, I, I think the first thing is that the legislation and some parts has specific provisions for employees and it speaks to the workplace.

Um, and so the precedent set there in terms of recognizing that, um, within, uh, the exposure to certain hazards and, and, and work, um, that the specific cover mentioned in a c around, uh, mental health, um, mucus scle injuries, that sort of thing, and, and certain cancers.

Um, so if that applies to the paid employees, um, the volunteers that are doing exactly the same work because of the nature of their engagement with FES that aren't qualified because they're not employees, but the, the Fine Emergency Act and the government at the time contemplating contemplated, um, establishing funds with volunteers in mind to be of a nature of employees, but engaged rather than employed.

So to be treated equally like their paid counterparts. And that's what's unique about the fire and emergency arrangement. And, and while we understand the, the potential scope creep in terms of other emergency services, I, I don't think it would necessarily be that, that extensive, uh, you mentioned paramedics, but there's also, uh, red Cross, there's surf life saving.

Yeah. We understand that. But, but, but given there are other interventions to prevent this harm, you know, with, with psychological support and all the rest of it, it doesn't necessarily mean that there's gonna be a huge amount of cases. In fact, you know, um, it's, it's probably not that widespread, but when it does happen, people are left without cover.

And, and the, the particular case in Kingston with Mr. Ottley is a very good example of that, where, you know, he, he's probably not gonna work again. And, um, and yet he's provided all of this support to the community, uh, for these years. So we think that there is a clear case, um, because the precedents actually set in the Foreign Emergency Act, New Zealand, um, to, to FE funds as required as a crown entity actually to apply the principles of a good employer to the volunteers as if they were employees.

So that speaks to the intention of government in the day. Mm-hmm. She was brought cross party support for that. Yeah. But it was also set up in a time when they didn't have mental health as part of a CC No, that act. Um, Catherine, I really appreciate the role of volunteer, uh, firefighters across my electorate.

I think we have eight volunteer fire brigades and try and go to many of the, uh, celebration and recognition e evenings. We've got another one coming up for the Harvard of Fire Brigade. Um, in a couple weeks time, has there been any conversation with FE around if this was to occur, how, um, FE could pay the, uh, essentially component that they would pay for paid firefighters, um, to a CC?

Because as you'd appreciate for in the employee realm, um, businesses, organizations pay into the scheme. And while I appreciate that volunteer firefighters don't earn a salary, um, has there been any conversation around how there could be a calculation by funds of the a CC levy?

It would pay for its, um, volunteer, uh, firefighters into the scheme if something like this was to go ahead. Mm-hmm. Uh, no direct conversations with FES as of yet, but FES presented a submission on this petition. Yep. Uh, and they did allude to conversations being needed around funding options.

So there's Recognition. So they're not, they're not, Unwin Hasn't happened yet. Yeah. Correct. Mm-hmm. Um, grant, Yeah, I, um, I really respect and appreciate all the work. Mm-hmm. Because it's so close to State Highway. Um, so is, is it having an impact? Is this having an impact on the ability to recruit volunteers? Are you seeing any direct correlation? This issue is becoming more widely known. Volunteers are understanding the personal risk that they're taking on, and the more it becomes well known, the more this will impact the decisions that they make.

Um, I find it pretty unreasonable that we would put people into this position where they're expected and required, um, but then also put them in a position where they are financially vulnerable, where their livelihoods are potentially at stake, should they, should they suffer through the course of this unpaid work.

Um, it, it seems a very unreasonable place to leave people. Thank I really appreciate. Thank you. C can I add to your ques Yeah. That answer? Yeah, please, please.
